title: Tiny Heroes Beat 'Bubble Boy Disease'
story: Doctors at St. Jude Children's Research Hospital in Memphis made an amazing breakthrough on April 17, 2019. They found a way to cure 'bubble boy disease' using a special treatment made from parts of HIV. Ten brave babies with this rare condition, which weakens the immune system, were successfully treated. The doctors used gene therapy to fix the babies' cells, helping them fight off germs like never before.

follow_up_question: Why do you think this discovery is important for children with rare diseases?

title: Giant Shipworm: The Living Pipe Monster
story: Imagine a clam as long as a child! In 2017, scientists made a jaw-dropping discovery in the Philippines on April 17. They found the first living giant shipworm, measuring an incredible three feet long. Despite its name, this creature isn't a worm at all - it's actually a rare type of clam that looks like a slimy, black tube. This bizarre animal had only been known from fossils before, making its discovery a thrilling moment for marine biologists worldwide.

title: Century-Old Message in a Bottle Found
story: A mysterious bottle washed up on a German beach, holding secrets from the past. In 2015, beachgoers on Amrum Island discovered a 108-year-old message in a bottle. The UK Marine Biological Association had tossed it into the sea on April 17, way back in 1907. Scientists used these floating time capsules to study ocean currents. This lucky find connected the present with history, showing how patient the sea can be in delivering its mail.

activity_choices: On a beach on Amrum Island
In the UK Marine Biological Association
At the bottom of the ocean
activity_answer: On a beach on Amrum Island

title: A Century-Old Message Washes Ashore
story: Imagine finding a 108-year-old letter from the sea! On April 17, 2015, Marianne Winkler discovered an extraordinary bottle on a German island beach. Inside was a message from 1906, dropped by British scientist George Parker Bidder. This sea-mail journey lasted over a century, drifting in the North Sea before reaching land. It's a reminder that the ocean holds many surprises, connecting the past to the present in unexpected ways.
